context: Current closing price of Southern Company (SO): $94.51 USD as of 2026-04-17.
justification_text:
My point estimates were anchored primarily to the prevailing price level at the time of the forecast, with only modest drift built in across horizons. For the 1-day estimate, I leaned heavily on short-term momentum and mean-reversion tendencies, expecting very little deviation from the last observed price given the absence of any imminent scheduled catalysts. The 1-week estimate incorporates a slight upward drift reflecting a mild positive bias in the broader market trend at the time, while the 1-month estimate extends that drift further but with a larger adjustment to account for the compounding uncertainty over a longer window. In all three cases, the point estimates are essentially centered near the current price with only small directional tilts, reflecting my view that near-term macro conditions were neither strongly bullish nor bearish.

The confidence interval widths were shaped primarily by realized historical volatility at the relevant time scale. The 1-day interval is comparatively tight because intraday and overnight price movements for a liquid asset tend to be constrained in the absence of breaking news. As the horizon extends to one week, the interval widens to reflect the accumulation of daily volatility and the increased probability of a scheduled economic release, earnings announcement, or geopolitical development materially shifting sentiment. By one month, the interval is nearly twice as wide as the 1-day range, capturing the reality that a month contains many potential inflection points, and that regime changes in volatility itself become a meaningful risk.

Several specific risks could push outcomes outside my stated intervals. On the upside, a surprise dovish pivot from a major central bank, unexpectedly strong economic data, or a sudden resolution of an ongoing geopolitical conflict could drive prices well above the upper bounds, particularly at the 1-week and 1-month horizons. On the downside, a credit event, sharp deterioration in risk appetite, or a hawkish policy surprise could breach the lower bounds. Liquidity shocks — such as a flash crash or forced institutional deleveraging — represent tail risks that are particularly hard to price into a symmetric confidence interval and could produce moves outside my stated ranges even within a single trading day.

Finally, I want to acknowledge that my stated 90% confidence intervals reflect modeled uncertainty and carry their own estimation error. If the underlying volatility regime shifts — for instance, if implied volatility spikes sharply after my forecast was made — the actual coverage of my intervals could be materially lower than 90%. Model risk, in other words, is itself an unquantified layer of uncertainty sitting on top of the intervals I provided.
